data.title: Bitcoin underperformed gold, but the golden age of the crypto economy has only just begun.
data.description:
Author: Ryan Watkins Translator: Deep Tide TechFlow

Deep Tide Introduction: In 2026, the crypto economy is undergoing its most critical transformation in eight years. This article delves into how the market can achieve a "soft landing" from the excessive expectations of 2021 and gradually establish a valuation framework based on cash flow and real-world use cases.

The author explains the pain of the past four years through the "Red Queen effect" and points out that with the relaxation of US regulations and the explosion of enterprise applications, crypto assets are shifting from cyclical speculation to long-term trend growth.

Faced with a global crisis of trust and currency devaluation, this is not only an industry recovery but also the rise of a parallel financial system. For investors deeply involved in Web3, this is not only a reshaping of perceptions but also an undervalued, cross-cycle entry opportunity.

The full text is as follows: Key Takeaways: This asset class prematurely priced in expectations in 2021; since then, valuations have been undergoing a rational correction, and the valuations of quality assets are now more reasonable. With the easing of the US regulatory environment, the issues of token alignment and value capture have finally turned around, making tokens more attractive investments. The growth of the crypto economy is shifting from cyclical to secular, and the industry has already generated some valuable use cases beyond Bitcoin. The winning blockchains are solidifying their status as the standard for both startups and large enterprises, and are becoming a hub for some of the world's fastest-growing businesses. With altcoins experiencing a four-year bear market and market sentiment at rock bottom, the multi-year opportunities of top projects are being mispriced by the market, and few analysts are considering exponential growth in their modeling. While top projects may thrive in the next era of the crypto economy, increased pressure to deliver on expectations and intensified competition from enterprises will weed out weaker players. Nothing is more powerful than the idea that "the time is ripe," and the crypto economy has never felt so unstoppable. In my eight years in the industry, the crypto economy is undergoing the biggest transformation I've ever seen. Institutions are accumulating capital, while pioneering cypherpunks are diversifying their wealth. Businesses are preparing for S-curve growth, while disillusioned industry-native developers are leaving. Governments are navigating the global financial transformation toward blockchain, while short-term traders remain concerned about the chart lines. Emerging markets are celebrating financial democratization, while American cynics lament that it's nothing more than a casino game. There have been many recent articles discussing "which period of history today's crypto economy most resembles." Optimists compare it to the period after the dot-com bubble burst, arguing that the era of speculation is over and long-term winners like Google and Amazon will emerge and climb the S-curve. Pessimists compare it to emerging markets, such as some markets in the 2010s, suggesting that weak investor protection and long-term capital shortages could lead to poor asset price performance, even as the industry thrives. Both perspectives have merit. After all, history is the best guide for investors besides experience. However, analogies can only offer limited insights. We also need to understand the crypto economy within its own macroeconomic and technological context. The market is not a single entity—it is composed of many interconnected yet distinct characters and stories. Here is my best assessment of where we are in the past and where we are headed. The Red Queen’s Cycle: “Right now, here you see, you have to run like crazy just to stay in the same place. If you want to go somewhere else, you have to run at least twice as fast!” — Lewis Carroll. In many ways, expectations are the only thing that matters in financial markets. Exceeding expectations leads to higher prices; failing to meet expectations leads to lower prices. Over time, expectations swing like a pendulum, and long-term returns are often negatively correlated with them. In 2021, the crypto economy overextended expectations to a far greater extent than most people realized. In some ways, this overheating was obvious, such as DeFi blue chips trading at 500 times price-to-sales ratios (P/S multiples), or eight smart contract platforms at the time having valuations exceeding $100 billion. Not to mention the metaverse and NFT hype. But the chart that best reflects this soberly is the Bitcoin/Gold ratio. Despite significant progress, the price of Bitcoin against gold has not reached a new high since 2021 and has actually been declining. Who would have thought that in Trump's so-called global "crypto capital," after the listing of the most successful ETF in history, and amidst the systemic devaluation of the dollar, Bitcoin's success as digital gold would be less than it was four years ago? As for other assets, the situation is much worse. Most projects entered this cycle with a series of structural problems that exacerbated the challenges of dealing with extreme expectations: The revenue of most projects is cyclical and predicated on continuously rising asset prices; Regulatory uncertainty hindered institutional and corporate participation; Dual ownership structures led to a mismatch of interests between equity insiders and public market token investors; Lack of disclosure standards resulted in information asymmetry between project teams and the community; and the lack of a shared valuation framework led to excessive volatility and a lack of a fundamental price floor. The combination of these problems caused most tokens to continuously "bleed," with only a few even managing to reach their 2021 highs. This has had a profound psychological impact, as few things in life are more frustrating than persistent, unrewarded effort. This disappointment is particularly acute for speculators and opportunists who saw cryptocurrency as the easiest path to wealth. Over time, this struggle has fueled widespread burnout across the industry. Of course, this is a healthy development. Mediocre effort should not consistently produce extraordinary results as it has in the past. The era before 2022, when even "vaporware" could create enormous wealth, is clearly unsustainable. Nevertheless, the silver lining is that the aforementioned issues are now widely understood, and prices have reflected these expectations. Today, aside from Bitcoin, few crypto natives are willing to discuss any long-term fundamental arguments. After four painful years, the asset class now possesses the necessary conditions to once again surprise the market.

The Enlightened Crypto Economy

As mentioned earlier, the crypto economy entered this cycle with many structural problems. Fortunately, everyone is now aware of this, and many of these problems are gradually becoming history.

First, besides digital gold, many use cases have shown compound growth, and many more are in the process of transformation.

First, besides digital gold, many use cases have shown compound growth, and many more are in the process of transformation.

Over the past few years, the crypto economy has produced: Peer-to-peer internet platforms: enabling users to transact and execute contractual relationships without government or corporate intermediaries. Digital dollars: providing a cheap and reliable currency to billions of people, stored and transferred anywhere on Earth with internet access. Permissionless exchanges: enabling anyone, anywhere, to trade top global assets across any asset class in a transparent venue, 24/7. Novel derivative instruments: such as event contracts and perpetual swaps, providing society with valuable predictive insights and more efficient price discovery, respectively. Global collateral markets: Enabling users to access credit without restrictions through a transparent, automated infrastructure, thereby substantially reducing counterparty risk. Democratized asset creation platforms: Enabling individuals and institutions to issue publicly tradable assets at extremely low costs. Open funding platforms: Enabling anyone in the world to raise funds for their business and overcome local economic limitations. Physical infrastructure networks (DePIN): Distributing operations to independent operators through crowdsourced capital, creating more scalable and resilient infrastructure. This is not an exhaustive list of all the value use cases the industry has built to date. The key point is that many of these use cases are demonstrating real value and are continuously growing regardless of the price movements of crypto assets. Meanwhile, as regulatory pressure eases and founders become increasingly aware of the cost of misalignment, dual equity-token models are being revised. Many existing projects are consolidating assets and revenue into a single token, while others are explicitly separating on-chain revenue for token holders from off-chain revenue for equity holders. Furthermore, with the maturation of third-party data providers, disclosure practices are improving, reducing information asymmetry and enabling better analytics. Meanwhile, the market is reaching a growing consensus on a simple and time-tested principle: aside from rare stores of value like Bitcoin (BTC) and Ethereum (ETH), 99.9% of assets need to generate cash flows. As more fundamental investors enter the asset class, these frameworks will only be reinforced, and rationality will increase accordingly. In fact, given enough time, the concept of "autonomous ownership of on-chain cash flows" could be understood as a paradigm shift on par with "autonomous digital stores of value." After all, when in history have you ever been able to hold digital bearer assets that autonomously pay you from anywhere on Earth every time a program is used? Against this backdrop, the emerging blockchains are gradually emerging as the monetary and financial cornerstone of the internet. Over time, the network effects of Ethereum, Solana, and Hyperliquid have intensified, thanks to their growing ecosystems of assets, applications, businesses, and users. Their permissionless design and global distribution have made applications on their platforms among the fastest-growing businesses in the world, boasting unparalleled capital efficiency and revenue turnover. In the long run, these platforms are likely to underpin the Total Potential Market (TAM) for Financial Superapps, an area that almost all leading fintech companies are currently vying for. Against this backdrop, it's no surprise that Wall Street and Silicon Valley giants are pushing their blockchain initiatives at full speed. A wave of new product announcements is emerging every week, covering everything from tokenization to stablecoins and everything in between. It's noteworthy that, unlike the pre-crypto era, these efforts are not experiments, but production-ready products, and most are built on public blockchains rather than isolated private systems. As the lagged effects of regulatory changes continue to permeate the system in the coming quarters, these activities will only accelerate. With increased clarity, businesses and institutions can finally shift their focus from "Is this legal?" to how blockchain can expand revenue opportunities, reduce costs, and unlock new business models. Perhaps one of the most telling signs of the current situation is that few industry analysts are building models for exponential growth. Anecdotal evidence suggests that many of my sell-side and buy-side colleagues are hesitant to consider annual growth rates above 20%, fearing it might seem overly optimistic. After four years of pain and with valuations now reset, it's time to ask ourselves: what if all this really did achieve exponential growth? What if "daring to dream" paid off again? Twilight Hour "Light a candle, and you cast a shadow." — Ursula LeGuin On a cool autumn day in 2018, before starting another tiring day at investment banking, I walked into an elderly professor's office, hoping to talk to him about everything related to blockchain. After I sat down, he recounted a conversation he had with a skeptical stock hedge fund manager who claimed that cryptocurrency was entering a nuclear winter and was a "solution to the problem." After giving me a crash course on unsustainable sovereign debt burdens and eroding institutional trust, he finally told me how he countered the skeptic: “Ten years from now, the world will thank us for building this parallel system.” While it's not even a decade away, his prediction seems remarkably prescient, as cryptocurrency increasingly appears to be a “ripe time” idea. In a similar spirit, and centrally thematically, this article aims to demonstrate that the world is still underestimating what's being built here. Most relevant for all investors, the multi-year opportunity of leading projects is being underestimated. This final part is crucial because while cryptocurrency may be unstoppable, your favorite token may actually be heading towards zero. The other side of cryptocurrency becoming unstoppable is that it's attracting fiercer competition, and the pressure to deliver results has never been greater. As I mentioned earlier, with the entry of institutions and corporations, they are likely to weed out many weaker players. This doesn't mean they'll win everything and monopolize the technology, but it does mean that only a few native players will emerge as the big winners in this global repositioning. The point here isn't cynicism. In all emerging technology sectors, 90% of startups fail. There will likely be more public failures in the coming years, but that shouldn't distract you from the bigger picture. Perhaps no technology is more zeitgeist-driven than cryptocurrency. Declining trust in institutions in developed societies, unsustainable government spending in the G7, blatant currency devaluation by the world's largest fiat currency issuer, the deglobalization and fragmentation of the international order, and a growing desire for a new system that is fairer than the old one—with software continuing to devour the world, AI becoming the latest accelerator, and younger generations inheriting wealth from the aging baby boomers, there's no better time for the crypto economy to emerge from its little bubble. While many analysts define this moment using classic frameworks like Gartner's hype cycle and Carlota Perez's "post-frenzy" phase, suggesting that the best returns are over and a more tedious instrumentation phase is on its way, the reality is far more interesting. The crypto economy isn't a single, fully mature market, but rather a collection of products and businesses on different adoption curves. More importantly, speculation doesn't disappear when a technology enters its growth phase; it simply fluctuates with shifting sentiment and the pace of innovation. Anyone telling you the age of speculation is over is probably just bored or doesn't understand history at all. It's reasonable to remain skeptical, but don't be cynical. We are reimagining how money, finance, and our most important economic institutions are governed. This should be challenging, but equally fun and exciting. Your next task is to figure out how best to utilize this emerging reality, not to write endless tweets arguing why it's all doomed to fail. Because through the fog of disillusionment and uncertainty, a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity will belong to those willing to gamble on the dawn of a new era rather than mourn the sunset of the old.

data.title: What exciting tracks will emerge in 2026?
data.description:
The core takeaway of this article:

1. Perpetual contracts, as on-chain exposures, liberate RWA from custody and issuance challenges:

Don't treat tokenization as the only entry point. A faster and more flexible path is to use perpetual contracts to create synthetic exposures,making almost any off-chain asset perpetual...

2. Exchange competition is shifting from matching to market structure design, with the core objective of protecting market makers and limited partners (LPs) from toxic traffic attacks: It is difficult to directly replicate stronger underlying protection on a general-purpose blockchain. Therefore, a more realistic opportunity is to drive the evolution of market structure through new structures (such as Prop-AMM). 3. After the prediction market enters the mainstream, the next stage of competitive advantage lies in the terminal aggregation layer: The current pain points are fragmented interfaces, weak tools, and fragmented liquidity; Whoever can create a cross-platform trading terminal with odds views, routing, position tracking, arbitrage, etc., is more likely to become the default entry point. 4. The key to the next generation of DeFi is the composability of perpetual markets, which combines trading, lending, and yield to improve capital efficiency: Perpetual contract exchanges have evolved from isolated scenarios to composable markets, allowing users to maintain leveraged positions while still earning interest on their collateral, and simultaneously hedging, earning yield, and leveraging without sacrificing liquidity. 5. For unsecured lending to succeed, it must integrate "on-chain reputation + off-chain data + scalable risk control model" into a unified system: The opportunities are significant, but the real hurdle lies in sustainable risk pricing and bad debt control; once this is achieved, DeFi may be able to directly challenge the traditional banking system in certain dimensions. 6. Privacy becomes a prerequisite for mainstream market adoption, with the goal of reducing exposure under verifiable conditions: The core logic is the existence of genuine demand—institutions and professional traders are unlikely to disclose their strategies on-chain for extended periods, and ordinary users are unwilling to disclose their complete financial history. 7. Trustworthiness and productivity are accelerating simultaneously in the AI ​​era: Human verification + AI proxy development: On the one hand, it is becoming increasingly difficult to distinguish between human-origin and AI-generated content, requiring a combination of biometrics, cryptographic signatures, and open standards; On the other hand, The development of smart contracts is about to enter a "Copilot era." AI agents package code generation, auditing, and monitoring modules, significantly lowering the barrier to entry for blockchain and expanding application supply. The following is a compiled full article for your reference. Every year, the forefront of the crypto industry is constantly changing. In 2025, we saw stablecoin infrastructure reshape the payment system, cross-chain proofs compress settlement times that used to take days to extremely short periods, prediction markets cross the chasm and achieve sustained mainstream adoption, and new DEX models create a market where "everything can be traded" on-chain. These breakthroughs pave the way for a new era: a group of ambitious teams working nights and weekends to create the next big opportunity in the crypto world. Comparing the beginning of the year to now, you'll see deeper liquidity, smarter privacy solutions, true interoperability, and on-chain finance tracks that complement AI. Regardless of where the price curve goes, we remain bullish on the future. Below are some of our team's most exciting ideas as we head towards 2026, and our answers to the frequently asked question—"What should I do next?". We believe the next wave of explosive growth companies and protocols will emerge from these tracks. I. RWA Perpetual Contracts: Making Everything Perpetual

With the resurgence of interest in Real-World On-Chain (RWA) assets, investors are seeking new forms of exposure. Perpetual contracts, as the most proven trading product in the crypto space, offer a structurally faster and more flexible path compared to "direct tokenization."Benefiting from recent improvements in decentralized perpetual contract infrastructure, RWA perpetual contracts can provide synthetic price exposure for off-chain assets through perpetual contracts.

We see this sector potentially developing in two directions: First, bringing more "non-mainstream" asset exposures onto the blockchain—because perpetual contracts don't require actual custody of the underlying assets; almost anything can form a market, from private companies to macroeconomic data releases, all potentially becoming perpetual. Second, as crypto and macro markets become increasingly intertwined, a more sophisticated trading community wants to express richer perspectives, not just simply going long on digital assets. This will create demand for on-chain macro asset exposures, allowing traders to hedge or position themselves using tools linked to crude oil, inflation breakeven, credit spreads, volatility, etc. II. Specialized Exchanges and Trading Terminals The rise of perpetual DEXs, application-specific chains, and Rollups highlights the critical importance of market structure design for building sustainable exchanges, especially in protecting market makers from toxic takers. While these new environments can embed protection mechanisms at the underlying level, replicating similar structures on general-purpose public chains often requires significant protocol upgrades, which is very difficult. Therefore, we are increasingly focusing on projects that can accelerate the evolution of on-chain market structures within a broader ecosystem. One emerging model is Prop-AMM on Solana: order book liquidity can only be traded through aggregators, thus isolating LPs from more predatory traffic. This "prop-driven" approach may significantly drive market structure innovation before underlying improvements arrive, and its application may extend beyond the spot market on the Solana chain. III. Trading Terminals in Prediction Markets Prediction markets have become one of the leading consumer-grade crypto applications and have crossed the chasm into mainstream adoption. However, today's prediction markets still face fragmentation issues similar to early DeFi: users need to switch between multiple interfaces, tool capabilities are limited, and liquidity pools are isolated from each other. Prediction market aggregators have also emerged. We expect them to become the dominant "interface layer," consolidating fragmented liquidity scattered across various platforms, totaling over $600 million, and providing a comprehensive, real-time odds overview across platforms. Imagine a trading terminal (similar to Axiom's interactive experience, but geared towards event contracts): offering advanced order types, filtering and charting, multi-platform routing and position tracking, cross-platform arbitrage insights, and other professional tools. IV. Next Generation DeFi 1. Composability of Perpetual Markets Perpetual contracts are evolving from isolated trading venues into composable DeFi markets, opening up new frontiers for capital efficiency. Leading perpetual contract exchanges like Hyperliquid and Lighter are pioneering integrations with lending protocols, allowing users to earn yields on collateral while maintaining leveraged positions. With perpetual DEX monthly trading volume reaching $1.4 trillion and a year-on-year growth of 300%, 2026 may see more protocols expanding the uses of perpetual contracts, allowing traders to hedge, earn yields, and leverage without sacrificing liquidity. 2. Uncollateralized Lending and Credit Credit-based, uncollateralized money markets represent the next frontier of DeFi. 2026 may see groundbreaking models combining on-chain reputation with off-chain data to achieve uncollateralized lending at scale. The market opportunity is enormous: The US alone has approximately $1.3 trillion in revolving uncollateralized credit lines, and crypto can compete for this market segment due to its higher capital efficiency and global accessibility. For those building this sector, the core challenge is designing scalable and sustainable risk management models. If successful, DeFi will become true financial infrastructure, even capable of surpassing the traditional banking system in some dimensions. 3. On-chain Privacy: Blockchain is known for its transparency, but mainstream adoption may be difficult to achieve if users cannot maintain privacy. Institutional and professional retail traders cannot trade with peace of mind if they continuously leak their strategies to competitors; ordinary users also generally do not want their complete financial history to be publicly displayed on the blockchain. We have seen a significant increase in developer investment in privacy, including privacy assets (such as Zcash), privacy-focused DeFi applications (such as private order books and private lending), and dedicated payment chains with privacy as a core mission. Whether built on dedicated privacy networks or superimposed on existing public chains using advanced cryptography (such as zero-knowledge proofs (ZKP), fully homomorphic encryption (FHE), multi-party computation (MPC), and trusted execution environments (TEE), these tools can reduce users' public exposure to malicious actors while maintaining verifiability. V. AI and Robotics 1. Robotics and Humanoid Data Acquisition As AI continues to expand, the market is turning its attention to the next technological frontier and a growing consensus is forming: robots may define the next stage of innovation. While many teams are moving in this direction, a key gap remains in training robots and embodied intelligent systems—available datasets are still limited and fragmented. One of the scarcest areas is refined physical interaction data, such as grasping, pressure, and multi-object manipulation involving deformable materials like fabrics and cables. While these challenges are not limited to the cryptographic field, incentivized data collection models like Decentralized Physical Infrastructure Networks (DePIN) may provide a feasible framework for the large-scale collection of high-quality physical interaction data, thereby accelerating the development and deployment of advanced robotic systems. 2. Human Proof We are approaching a tipping point: everything you see on any connected digital screen will be decoupled from real human origins and indistinguishable from AI-generated content. We believe that a combination of biometrics, cryptographic signatures, and open-source developer standards will be key to establishing human proof schemes, complementing AI in new human-computer interface models. Worldcoin is at the forefront of identifying and combating this problem. We also hope to support more pathways to collectively address this increasingly complex problem space. 3. Using AI for On-Chain Development and Security Smart contract development is about to usher in its "GitHub Copilot moment." In 2026, AI agents may further lower the barrier to on-chain development: founders without a technical background can launch on-chain businesses within hours (instead of months), while the agent is responsible for smart contract code generation, security auditing, and continuous monitoring. The opportunity lies in building a proxy-based toolchain that makes smart contract development and security and risk management as easy to learn as modern web development, potentially triggering a "Cambrian explosion" in on-chain applications and experiences. Looking ahead to 2026, we are excited about the builders who dare to take big bets and drive the on-chain economy forward. These ideas reflect areas we believe have great potential, but the most exciting projects often come from unexpected places.

data.title: Dogecoin Slides as Meme Coins Crack Under Volatility, Keeping Traders in Sell-the-Rally Mode
data.description:
Dogecoin’s volatility hangover deepens as traders stay firmly on the sidelines

Dogecoin slipped roughly 2% to around $0.125, extending a broader pullback across meme coins as renewed liquidation pressure swept through the crypto market. The move pushed DOGE below key short-term support near $0.1254, reinforcing a bearish structure that has defined recent trading and underscoring how vulnerable high-beta assets remain during periods of heightened volatility.

The decline unfolded on elevated volume, signaling active repositioning rather than fading interest. During the U.S. session, trading activity surged well above average as sellers pressed the token to an intraday low near $0.123, confirming a pattern of lower highs and lower lows. While DOGE attempted brief rebounds, those moves were quickly sold into, reflecting a market still firmly operating in sell-the-rally mode.

This price behavior mirrors broader conditions across crypto markets, where risk appetite has thinned and leverage is being unwound. Meme coins, which tend to amplify both upside and downside moves, have been among the hardest hit. Unlike earlier speculative surges driven by social momentum, the current environment is dominated by caution, with traders prioritizing capital preservation over aggressive positioning.

BITCOIN IS SETTING UP A DOUBLE FAKEOUT.

First:
break above resistance (~$96k)… then rejected.

Now:
price is back at the rising trendline support.

Bullish fakeout case:$BTC reclaims the trendline and squeezes back up.

Bearish case:
We likely revisit the range lows.

Markets… pic.twitter.com/725fghbDrm

— Merlijn The Trader (@MerlijnTrader) January 23, 2026

From a technical perspective, DOGE is now trapped between well-defined resistance near $0.126–$0.127 and a fragile support band around $0.124–$0.123. Short-term oversold signals are beginning to appear, but weakness on higher timeframes suggests that any bounce is likely to be corrective rather than the start of a sustained reversal. A failure to hold above the $0.123 area would leave $0.12 and potentially $0.115 as the next downside levels in focus.

For traders, the message remains one of patience rather than prediction. As long as DOGE remains capped below the $0.126 zone, rallies are likely to attract sellers rather than fresh demand. Holding above current support could allow for sideways consolidation and base-building, but a meaningful shift in tone would require the token to reclaim and hold key resistance levels—something it has struggled to do since the latest breakdown.

$Doge/monthly#Dogecoin 🔥 pic.twitter.com/rkDcmZiB66

— Trader Tardigrade (@TATrader_Alan) January 23, 2026

In the absence of DOGE-specific catalysts, price action continues to be driven by broader market forces. With volatility elevated across cryptocurrencies and liquidity conditions tightening, Dogecoin’s recent slide highlights how speculative tokens remain highly sensitive to shifts in sentiment, leaving traders navigating a market defined more by defense than momentum.

data.title: Besides Polymarket and Kalshi, who else is involved in the prediction market?
data.description:
Author: Will Owens, Research Analyst, Galaxy Digital; Translation: @jinsecaijingxz

Prediction markets have entered a new phase of mainstream attention and capital formation. This niche field, once merely on-chain speculation, is rapidly developing into one of the fastest-growing segments in the financial industry.

Polymarket's remarkable growth (record-breaking daily trading volume, a valuation of $9 billion, and recognition from traditional finance) and the success of Kalshi (topping the free financial app charts on the iOS App Store) together demonstrate that the product-market fit that prediction markets have long sought to achieve has been realized.

The history of prediction markets can be traced back to at least the 16th century, when true Renaissance elites were already betting on papal elections. The prototype of modern prediction markets originated from the idea of ​​George Mason University economist Robin Hansen.

Hansen's early research on information markets and "voting markets" clarified how financial incentives could be used to aggregate dispersed information, outperforming polls, forecasts, or expert committees. His ideas laid the conceptual foundation for prediction markets—viewing them as probabilistic price discovery tools, rather than simple betting products. This is precisely the market positioning strategy of Kalshi and Polymarket.

This is not gambling, but prediction. Is it really?

Currently, the competitive landscape of prediction markets is expanding. A new wave of entrants is accelerating experiments in product design and liquidity incentive mechanisms.

Decentralized finance protocols are also exploring the potential integration of prediction assets. Theoretically, binary outcome tokens (Yes/No tokens) can function as composable financial instruments outside their native prediction markets. For example, "Yes" tokens linked to election results could serve as collateral for long Bitcoin positions in perpetual contract DEXs. Meanwhile, the increasing clarity of US regulation of event contracts has opened the door for the parallel expansion of regulated platforms and fully on-chain venues. See Polymarket's newly launched US-compliant mobile app (currently only supporting sports betting). Since the 2024 US presidential election, prediction markets seem to have become central to all discussions about information discovery and on-chain finance. More and more companies are launching their own prediction market products, and capital inflows are surging. The field is undergoing a major transformation. This report will outline the current state of prediction markets, the core innovations driving their evolution, and why the next phase may blur the lines between event markets and derivatives. Key Takeaways: Prediction markets have crossed a significant chasm, entering a stage of mainstream recognition and capital formation: Polymarket and Kalshi are among the fastest-growing consumer finance products; Polymarket has surpassed 1.6 million cumulative unique users; the Kalshi mobile app ranks first among iOS financial apps. The increasing clarity of US regulations is driving the simultaneous expansion of prediction market models domestically and internationally. Prediction markets are evolving from simple betting tools into fundamental financial components. Leveraged prediction markets may develop into event risk hedging products similar to perpetual contracts. Artificial intelligence will reshape the pricing mechanism, use cases, and cognitive methods of prediction markets. Liquidity remains a core constraint in this field. Brief Explanation: Many readers may already understand the operating mechanism and core value of prediction markets—extracting effective signals from a sea of ​​information. For those who are still unfamiliar, a brief explanation follows. Taking the question "Will the Buffalo Bills win the Super Bowl?" as an example: Users can purchase "Yes" or "No" shares. If the Bills win, each "Yes" share pays $1, otherwise $0; the settlement method for "No" shares is the opposite. The share price reflects the probability of the event occurring. If the "yes" share trades at $0.22 and the "no" share at $0.78, it indicates that the market believes the Bills have a 22% probability of winning the Super Bowl. Theoretically, the greater the trading volume and liquidity these markets attract, the more accurately their prices can reflect collective wisdom and form a reliable prediction of the true probability. One of the most underrated use cases for prediction markets is hedging before cryptocurrency listings. For example, using a fully diluted valuation on the first day of a token's listing as the basis for settlement, the question "What will be the fully diluted valuation of Monad on its first day of listing?" on Polymarket is a typical case. For traders and participants holding MON quotas or pre-sale positions, these tools often provide a purer hedging solution than the pre-sale "super perpetual contracts" traded on Hyperliquid, which aim to obtain leveraged directional exposure to MON. This is primarily because they effectively mitigate short squeeze risk. As we previously pointed out, while pre-sale perpetual contracts allow traders to establish leveraged directional exposure before token issuance, they also introduce liquidation risk. Even if a trader's long-term judgment or hedging direction is correct, a coordinated short squeeze can trigger forced liquidation before the hedging event occurs. The Polymarket pre-sale market completely avoids this failure mode. Since there is no leverage or liquidation mechanism, the only possibility of losing principal is the failure to meet preset conditions at settlement. The trade is either paid out or not. From a risk management perspective, this makes the Polymarket pre-sale market closer to binary options with discrete outcomes than continuous derivatives. For example, consider a Lighter airdrop recipient: if they expect to receive tokens upon mainnet launch and want to hedge against downside risk on the first day of trading, the Polymarket market, based on FDV settlement on the first day of LIT token issuance, provides exposure unaffected by temporary price fluctuations or short squeezes. This hedging precisely addresses the core risk concerns of participants: specific valuation checkpoints at clearly defined timeframes (with verifiable and clear settlement criteria). This doesn't mean the Polymarket pre-sale market is risk-free. Participants must thoroughly understand the settlement criteria, and order book liquidity must meet position size requirements. However, these crypto pre-sale markets typically offer sufficient liquidity for most traders. 2. Current Landscape of Prediction Markets Prediction markets have evolved from a niche on-chain novelty into the fastest-growing vertical in the cryptocurrency space. While the current market size is far from its theoretical potential, the foundations supporting a larger-scale system are being built. Economist Hansen recently described prediction markets as still in a "very early stage" compared to the world he envisioned in the 1990s. He warned that regulatory or cultural backlash against gambling practices, insider trading, or celebrity-driven markets could stall or delay this trajectory for years. Galaxy Research predicts a federal investigation into insider trading in prediction markets in 2026. However, this is not a black-and-white issue. While the "insider trading problem" is often criticized, proponents (including Hansen) argue that insider trading is a "function, not a flaw" of prediction markets because it creates economic incentives for those holding significant, non-public information to disclose it to the public. In fact, companies have attempted to establish internal prediction markets where employees bet on product release dates, aiming to reveal the truth rather than cater to management's preferred information. The current landscape is primarily defined by two dominant players. Despite other participants (including Opinion, backed by Binance founder Changpeng Zhao's YZi Labs), Kalshi and Polymarket remain the undisputed leaders. Polymarket is the most compelling proof that event contracts can achieve true consumer-scale performance. It is both a trading platform and a real-time, probabilistic information stream. Its prices are now considered "authoritative figures," much like odds, poll averages, or implied interest rates in traditional markets. When discussing whether an event will occur, "check out Polymarket" has become a common phrase.

Since the 2024 US presidential election cycle, two forces have driven the platform's growth.

... First, the inclusion of "signaling markets" (political, macroeconomic, and high-profile real-world events) solidifies Polymarket's role as a probability barometer. Second, the expansion into non-political categories such as crypto-native events and sports not only increases the coverage of tradable assets but also improves user retention by offering daily tradable instruments (rather than just major news cycles). This category expansion is crucial because prediction markets are ultimately constrained by cadence: the more frequently markets are listed, settled, and attention cycles occur, the more "liquidity duration" the platform can accumulate. Therefore, Polymarket has become a global leader in crypto-native prediction markets. Its daily nominal trading volume often reaches tens of millions of dollars, especially during political and macroeconomic events. The platform has evolved beyond a trading venue into a real-time information stream for observers. Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), in announcing a strategic investment of up to $2 billion, stated its plans to explore the commercial development of Polymarket data (this investment valued the platform at approximately $9 billion, making founder Shane Copeland the world's youngest self-made billionaire), further solidifying this role. Driven by viral market trends and the expansion of non-political categories such as sports and crypto-native events, user adoption accelerated significantly after the 2024 US election cycle. In December, after receiving approval from the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission to become a federally regulated intermediary event contract trading venue, the platform launched its first real-money trading mobile application for US users. Currently, the application is only available to US users in the sports category and is being gradually rolled out through an iOS candidate list; an Android version will be released later. While its US operations are currently limited to the sports sector, Polymarket has stated its intention to expand into the sponsored and potentially election-related markets as the regulatory environment evolves. This marks Polymarket's substantial return to the US market after its 2022 settlement with the CFTC (which prohibited it from doing business with Americans), not to mention the brief investigation it faced towards the end of the Biden administration. Throughout the ecosystem, Polymarket and Kalshi remain core sources of mainstream funding. Kalshi dominates activity in the regulated US market, while Opinion is also beginning to generate substantial weekly trading volume. The chart below shows the weekly notional spot trading volume on each platform.

Kalshi：

Crypto natives often scoff at Kalshi because of its "off-chain operation" or "non-crypto company" status. In reality, the company's stance is pragmatic rather than idealistic.

... The team initially hoped to build an on-chain platform to gain advantages in transparent settlement and composability, but regulatory uncertainty in the United States several years ago made it difficult to proceed without jeopardizing the core business. Kalshi ultimately opted for a fully off-chain architecture, aiming to launch compliant and scalable products first, and expecting to gradually add "on-chain" integrations at the appropriate time. This long-term openness is crucial because numerous "bridge" integrations between Kalshi and crypto-native products have emerged in the past few months, such as: DFlow tokenization (detailed below), Phantom wallet integration, Jupiter prediction market integration, and Coinbase platform integration. These workarounds have successfully channeled on-chain liquidity and developer innovation to Kalshi without attempting to migrate entire regulated exchanges to the blockchain. Kalshi simply allows builders to encapsulate or interface their contracts in a way that is understandable to DeFi and crypto-native users, thus simultaneously accessing all on-chain liquidity. The most striking point in our conversation with the Kalshi team was their unwavering focus on liquidity as a core constraint. Kalshi is now a mainstream product—its app topping the app store charts is proof of that. However, most markets still lack sufficient liquidity to allow established capital like hedge funds to comfortably position themselves (with a few exceptions, where Wall Street giant Susquehanna International acts as a market maker). Kalshi envisions "next-stage growth" not as requiring entirely new products, but rather as a slight improvement in the baseline liquidity of core markets. This focus permeates all aspects, including internal sub-programs that improve execution quality as trading volume increases. This liquidity-first strategy also explains the significant role of the sports sector in Kalshi's business. Users will always trade what they want to trade, and sports is often the largest market. In short, the sports sector boasts the broadest event coverage, consistent frequency of occurrence, and clear settlement standards (even if fans boo the referee, the score remains uncontested). No other category boasts comparable breadth and mindshare. Despite Kalshi's grand vision of encompassing all event futures (its name means "everything" in Arabic), sports remain a natural liquidity magnet.

Completely excluding sports data and observing weekly trading volume for "non-sports" categories provides a more valuable analytical perspective. Excluding the sports sector, Polymarket remains the more dominant trading venue for political/macroeconomic fund flows.

... In other words, non-sports trading volume is the fairest comparable dimension for measuring the "signal market" because it strips away the ecosystem layer that attempts to evolve into financial infrastructure (rather than mass entertainment products). Customer acquisition and distribution, along with user experience, are two other key levers emphasized by Kalshi. The team stresses that direct retail traffic is a crucial moat: by continuously acquiring and retaining consumers, liquidity can be gradually accumulated through "scale dominance"—something often difficult for offshore exchanges and weak on-chain order books to achieve. This is why Kalshi closely monitors the marketing strategies of sports betting giants like FanDuel and DraftKings, and continuously invests heavily in product iteration. Currently, a large-scale user experience upgrade is underway, including designing differentiated interfaces for traders of varying skill levels (beginner, intermediate, and advanced). This is similar to Coinbase's "easy" and "advanced" modes in its mobile app (or analogous to a ski resort offering multi-level runs from children's slopes to black diamond tracks). The underlying logic is simple: the easier a prediction market is to trade, the more practical it becomes; and a more convenient trading experience will attract more liquidity, making prices more reliable—essentially a self-reinforcing growth flywheel. Kalshi's roadmap views the ecosystem as a modular structure. The team envisions a future where different front-ends and tools serve different user groups: native apps, professional terminals, Telegram bots, and customized interfaces will all drive the maturity of the prediction market trading ecosystem. We've witnessed the exact same trajectory in the meme space: initially, traders manually exchanged through Raydium pools, then BONKbot, followed by Trojan, then GMGN, and finally Axiom launched the "ultimate" meme trading terminal. At this stage, Kalshi's primary task remains executing its core strategy: increasing liquidity and trading volume by an order of magnitude. 3. The Next Generation Ecosystem Today, every key opinion leader on crypto Twitter seems to be wearing a badge of approval from Kalshi or Polymarket. These companies are indeed investing heavily in inviting influential figures to promote their products. The current market narrative is that crypto traders are rotating from memes to prediction markets. As we discussed on the Galaxy Grid podcast, prediction markets may be more sustainable than memes (although users of the former cannot expect a single trade to turn $10 into $100,000). Against this backdrop, a new generation of prediction market platforms and tool ecosystems is taking shape. The following are some representative projects (not a complete list):

Opinion: A prediction market powered by YZi Labs

Augur: A reboot of an early Ethereum prediction market

Lightcone: An influence marketplace platform

Azuro

PrediX

Limitless: Focused on fast-expiring markets

Myriad Markets

Noise.xyz: For trading attention, narratives, and mindshare

Hilo

Gondor: A lending DeFi protocol for Polymarket positions

Space: Leveraged prediction markets on Solana (up to 10x) Melee: A hybrid of Polymarket and Pump.fun, using bound curve liquidity. Fliprbot: User Interface and Trading Terminal Polycule: Telegram Trading Bot MetaDAO: Market-Driven Governance Protocol Cue.market: Popular Culture, Fashion, and Entertainment Bullpen: Adding Polymarket Terminal Functionality Polyoptions: Polymarket Share Options Most newcomers are not simply copying the Polymarket or Kalshi models, but are exploring new infrastructures, such as: Event Outcome Leverage left;">Predicting Market Share Lending

  • Decision Market

  • Opinion Market

  • Multi-Outcome Unified Market

  • Influence Market

    • DFlow: Tokenizing Kalshi Based on Solana

    Another different but equally important direction is to directly tokenize off-chain prediction markets and integrate them into DeFi. As an infrastructure protocol, DFlow's prediction market API encapsulates regulated Kalshi event contracts into SPL tokens (the Ethereum ERC-20 token standard on Solana), enabling these positions to be traded and integrated into on-chain financial applications while maintaining off-chain settlement and compliance. Each Kalshi position thus becomes compatible with on-chain applications. At the technical level, DFlow employs a parallel liquidity procedure to connect off-chain Kalshi liquidity with on-chain trading intentions: users place orders on Solana, off-chain liquidity providers execute trades, and the protocol then mints or burns tokens representing the corresponding predicted market risk exposure. When the market settles, settlement funds flow back through the parallel liquidity procedure, and winning tokens can be exchanged for stablecoins. Gondor: Introducing Lending Functionality Gondor essentially builds a credit layer on top of Polymarket. Since each Polymarket position is an ERC-1155 token, these positions can be considered collateralized assets, allowing for leverage and risk management like other DeFi positions. As mentioned earlier, if the outcome is favorable to the holder, these positions can be exchanged for USDC at a 1:1 ratio. Gondor allows traders to deposit these ERC-1155 positions as collateral into a lending vault built on Morpho (a blue-chip lending protocol managing billions of dollars in deposits and boasting a long audited track record). Once deposited, traders can borrow up to 50% of the position's value in USDC. The borrowed funds flow directly back to the Polymarket platform and appear as a "cash balance" in the account. This allows traders to release idle equity that would otherwise be locked in profitable or market-valued positions, redeploying funds to new trades while maintaining risk exposure. It's important to note that not all markets are eligible. The most significant risk lies in illiquid markets (which are numerous) which may face targeted liquidations and market manipulation. The Gondor team manually reviews the Polymarkets supported by the lending protocol, sometimes even supporting only one-sided positions (e.g., only "yes" shares) if the corresponding liquidity is too scarce. The market whitelist review considers multiple factors: order book depth, clarity of settlement standards, and remaining settlement time. Each supported market is mapped to a specific lending vault, each with independent risk parameters, loan-to-value ratio caps, and lendable exposure limits. Lending capacity is limited by the deposit size of the Gondor vault and the liquidity depth of the underlying Polymarket order book. The principle for setting borrowing limits is that positions can be reasonably hedged or closed out in the underlying Polymarket.

    The maximum borrowing amount is set at a loan-to-value ratio of 50%, and the liquidation threshold is approximately 77% LTV. Positions established with maximum leverage will only trigger liquidation after the collateral value falls by approximately 35%.

    For example, suppose you hold 1,000 "Yes" shares in the "Will OpenAI release consumer hardware products before December 19th?" market, with each share valued at $0.60. If new information emerges that leads the market to believe the product release may be later than expected, your share price could drop to $0.39.

    At this point, your LTV will reach approximately 77%, triggering liquidation (and potentially resulting in loss of invested principal). The design of this liquidation mechanism is the most controversial aspect of such protocols. When a borrower's LTV exceeds the liquidation threshold, Gondor does not immediately seize and sell the collateral. Instead, it first purchases a position with the opposite outcome on Polymarket to hedge the risk, locking in a repayment path before disposing of the collateral. This order is deliberately designed: since the Polymarket order book operates off-chain, seizing the collateral before hedging would introduce delays and execution risks. After hedging, Gondor will seize 77% of the collateral, pairing and merging "yes" and "no" positions, and redeeming them at a price of 1 USDC per pair. The remaining 23% of the collateral can be withdrawn by the borrower after loan repayment. Currently, Gondor uses a centralized liquidation engine and plans to open liquidation to external participants once the protocol matures and liquidity improves. This design carries several risks. Most notably, Gondor is structurally forced to buy inverse positions during periods of sharp market repricing. In rapidly fluctuating or near-settlement markets, Polymarket order books are typically illiquid with wide spreads, where bid-ask spreads can be extremely broad. If Gondor is forced to intervene in the inverse market with market orders in such an environment, as a predictable liquidity demander, it may lock in unfavorable prices and be vulnerable to sandwich attacks or pre-trading by more agile bots. As long as the underlying market on which Gondor provides funding remains relatively shallow, there is a clear incentive for third-party manipulation. These risks can be mitigated by avoiding illiquid markets and reducing exposure through early closing of positions near settlement. Overall, Gondor clearly demonstrates the direction this space is heading. The team recently completed a $2.5 million pre-seed funding round, with participation from Castle Island Ventures, Maven 11, and Prelude, among others. Its very existence is a bet on "prediction market share becoming a standardized collateral asset class." For sophisticated traders, Gondor is pushing prediction markets further from a glamorous gambling front end towards macro derivative characteristics. Space: Leveraged Prediction Markets One of the most significant developments in prediction markets is the emergence of leveraged prediction markets. The surge in trading volume on perpetual contract DEXs clearly demonstrates the market's demand for leveraged financial instruments—perpetual futures contracts have become one of the most successful products in the crypto space. Users can leverage their positions to go long or short on assets, providing a fixed amount of collateral, and are liquidated when the price reaches a specific threshold, thus losing their collateral. The Space protocol on Solana allows users to gain event outcome risk exposure with up to 10x leverage by providing only a portion of the position value as collateral. A simple example in its documentation illustrates the working mechanism: Market: "Will the US government shut down before the end of the year?" "Yes" Share Price: $0.15 (Implicit Probability 15%) Trader buys 1000 "Yes" shares—normally requiring $150. With 5x leverage, only $30 margin (20% of the notional value) is needed. If the probability rises to 30%, the position value becomes $300, and the $30 margin yields a 500% return on equity (a profit of $150). If the probability drops to 13.33% ($0.13), the trader will be liquidated, losing the $30 margin. The leverage mechanism makes prices more sensitive to marginal information. Space also implements a unified multi-outcome market mechanism, where all possible outcomes of the same issue (e.g., who among five candidates will win the primary) share the same liquidity pool, rather than being split into separate contracts. This structure significantly reduces slippage, making pricing of complex events more efficient. This can be viewed as a perpetual contract exchange for event markets—a direction many market participants expect other platforms to explore. Perhaps Polymarket will eventually natively support leverage in its flagship global platform as well. The introduction of leverage significantly enhances the informational attributes of prediction markets. By allowing traders to express market beliefs with less capital, leveraged prediction markets accelerate the process of incorporating new information into prices. However, they also introduce common risks found in derivatives markets: the liquidation waterfall effect and path-dependent volatility near settlement. In illiquid or binary outcome scenarios, these dynamics can easily amplify market noise, much like amplifying signals. Similar to perpetual contract trading, the extent to which leveraged prediction markets can improve price discovery will ultimately depend on liquidity depth and the adaptive adjustment of risk parameters near settlement. **Artificial Intelligence and Prediction Markets:** Ethereum founder Vitalik Buterin offered several insightful observations in his 2024 paper, *Information Finance*, one of which is that artificial intelligence has the potential to greatly expand the feasible design space of prediction markets—markets that were previously too small to attract professional human traders—by participating in all micro-markets with high quality. A prime example is the Polymarket marketplace for "Winners of the Midwest Blockchain Conference Research Competition." This marketplace has an extremely thin order book, making it virtually unprofitable (a $20 buy order can boost the probability by about 40%). Human traders have virtually no incentive to price effectively because the profit opportunity is negligible. However, AI agents can evaluate each research proposal at low cost, score its competitiveness, analyze it against judges' historical preferences, and continuously update market prices. Crucially, the incentive to run these AI agents doesn't have to come solely from trading profits. In many cases, the agents may be funded by institutions or individuals who value market information output, with trading activity becoming a signal aggregation mechanism rather than an independent source of income. These micro-markets are precisely where human attention is scarce while AI attention is abundant; prediction markets here begin to resemble information engines rather than gambling products. AI reduces participation costs to near zero, enabling the market to generate effective signals even with small stakes. Imagine a scenario where thousands of micro-markets operate in parallel, each efficiently priced by AI agents. What about the user interface layer? Artificial intelligence could also become the user interface layer between users and prediction markets. Most people don't know which market to trade, how to sizing their positions, what related markets exist, or whether their perceived ideal trade is the best expression of their viewpoint. Furthermore, in our observation, the user experience of the Polymarket main platform is quite clunky—frequently displaying animated ribbons indicating successful trades. This issue has been widely criticized and will be gradually improved through Polymarket's own product iterations and the development of third-party prediction market terminals. In contrast, the Polymarket US mobile application (reportedly using off-chain transactions) offers a very smooth user experience. As prediction markets proliferate, users will increasingly rely on AI agents to translate their natural language-expressed opinions into optimal on-chain market exposures. Imagine telling an AI agent, "I believe Zcash will break $600 in the next three weeks." The AI ​​won't need to painstakingly search for answers like a human: "Will Zcash break $1000 by the end of the year?", "What price will Zcash reach by 2027?", or "Will Zcash break $500 before December 31st?". Instead, it can instantly determine: Are there event contracts on Polymarket that directly express this viewpoint? Is the pricing of the same outcome more attractive in the Kalshi market? Are there other prediction markets with better risk-reward ratios? Do leveraged prediction markets (such as Space) offer purer trading solutions? Do long perpetual contracts on DEXs like Hyperliquid offer better trading options? How should positions be allocated based on the user's risk tolerance and capital size? AI can act as the user's strategist (scanning relevant markets, assessing liquidity, identifying mispricing, and executing trades that best reflect the user's viewpoint). As the number of markets proliferates and becomes more granular, the cognitive load on humans will continue to increase. However, for AI, this complexity is an advantage, not a disadvantage—the more markets there are, the greater the optimization space for intelligent agents. Market effectiveness depends on maintaining its open capital depth. Without a deep order book, prices become unreliable and susceptible to manipulation. Markets of Influence: Markets of influence aim to address a fundamental information gap: current prediction markets cannot inform the value of an asset under specific event conditions. This information is not in a clearly discoverable form. While prediction markets reveal event probabilities and spot markets display prices, there is no mechanism to present the market's collective judgment on the valuation of an asset under specific event-bound conditions—for example, how Bitcoin would trade if the Federal Reserve cut interest rates by 75 or 50 basis points, or how Nvidia stock would perform if a candidate positing an AI threat wins a major election. In Influence Markets, users no longer trade probability odds using synthesized yes/no tokens, but instead trade the asset itself directly under specific conditions, expressing positions such as "I would buy BTC for $110,000 (a 10% premium to the market price) if and only if the Fed cuts interest rates by 75 basis points." This fundamentally enhances the market's information disclosure capabilities. We no longer need to maintain two separate markets for "Fed rate cut probability" and "BTC price," but instead directly obtain a discovery mechanism for "BTC price under the condition of a 75 basis point Fed rate cut." This concept can be extended to any asset-event pairing, such as "Google's stock price under the condition that GPT-6 is released before Gemini Series 4" or "Gold price under the condition that asteroid mining is achieved before 2030." The key difference is that the event itself and its impact on the company and asset are completely different things. While predicting the probability of events occurring in market aggregation markets, influence markets answer the next question: "What would this event mean for the company or asset?" This separation allows each type of market to specialize while forming a more complete set of information. Decision markets extend the mechanisms of influence markets from information disclosure to governance automation. These markets no longer merely present conditional valuations for individual decision-making, but directly and bindingly determine whether an organization takes action based on the outcome priced higher by the market. Decision markets originated from Hansen's 2000 working paper, "Should We Vote for Values, But Bet on Beliefs?" This mechanism has been implemented in practice through a "voting-based DAO," with total transaction volume in the related decision-making market reaching millions of dollars. In a typical setup, an organization proposes a decision (e.g., whether to issue 5% more tokens to fund a new product line), and the market trades in two conditional "states": passed and rejected. Each state assigns an independent value to the organization's tokens, and the token price becomes the market's objective function—the target the market seeks to optimize. If the token is priced higher in the "passed" state, the organization executes the decision; if the token is priced higher in the "rejected" state, the proposal is rejected and no action is taken. Market participants collectively decide which action maximizes the expected value, and their transactions are conditionally executed based on the final result. Galaxy Research has conducted in-depth analysis of these markets and related application organizations in its report on voting systems and their on-chain implementation, as well as in our annual forecasts for 2025 and 2026.

    Viewpoint Markets:

    Prediction markets anchor objective outcomes ("Will X occur?"), while "viewpoint markets" focus on subjective questions and narratives. These markets do not rely on hard external oracles or binary settlement rules, but rather operate as sentiment barometers.

    Many economically relevant issues in the crypto space, culture, and politics are often difficult to define with clear settlement standards. For example:

    Which Layer 2 is currently receiving the most attention?

    ... Following Powell's recent meeting, is market sentiment more bullish or bearish? These questions are important because they influence capital allocation but cannot be settled through deterministic oracles. Opinion markets (such as Noise.xyz) allow speculation on the narrative itself, rather than discrete outcomes. Opinion markets typically do not settle through deterministic oracles or binary settlement rules. Instead, they operate as continuous sentiment instruments, where prices reflect the market's collective view at a specific moment, rather than the eventual "correct" outcome. Traders profit if their bet on the opinion price rises and lose if it falls. In contrast, prediction markets are inherently constrained by settlement requirements. To be tradable, the question must be objectively verifiable, unambiguous, and verifiable by a reliable data source. For example, consider the following rules set by Polymarket for "What will Powell say at his December press conference?"

    4、Risk Warning

    Prediction markets are still in a relatively early stage, and the path from "viral products" to sustainable financial infrastructure is not yet clear.

    The biggest constraint at present is liquidity. Most markets have shallow order book depth and wide spreads, often requiring only a few thousand dollars to cause share prices to fluctuate by more than 10%. In such markets, probability data can be misleading, and market manipulation is a real risk. Only the most liquid markets can provide reliable probabilities.

    Theoretically, this problem will be resolved over time as market liquidity improves, as mentioned earlier, AI agents can also help price smaller markets effectively. However, insufficient liquidity is a current reality and will persist into 2026. Another risk lies in market construction and settlement logic. Prediction markets ultimately settle according to pre-defined rules; ambiguity in the definition or inconsistency between the market title and settlement standards can impair signal quality. We recently discussed this issue in our analysis of the Polymarket "Will the US invade Venezuela before…?" market—which sparked controversy due to inconsistencies between the title and settlement definition. Oracle issues are another risk point. Blockchain itself cannot perceive the real world and requires external infrastructure to transmit real-world information to the market. The reliability and governance models of these oracle mechanisms introduce additional risk nodes, especially when certain participants have economic incentives to act selfishly. The risk of regulatory backlash remains high, especially as platforms expand beyond sports. Even with compliant models, prediction markets could still face policy risks if they are characterized as encouraging harmful behavior or "betting on tragic events." Imagine the extreme case of manipulating prediction market settlements to the point of injury or death; as these products become more mainstream, the likelihood of attracting such behavior will increase. This sounds horrifying, but we have already witnessed similar behavior to manipulate token prices in pump.fun live streams. Unfortunately, humans can indeed resort to extreme measures for economic gain. Prediction markets are transforming from fringe speculative products into fundamental financial infrastructure. Event-linked contracts are increasingly exhibiting characteristics of derivatives, hedging instruments, collateralized assets, and information infrastructure. In the short term, progress will be driven more by liquidity accumulation than by novel market designs. Despite rapid growth, most prediction markets remain illiquid, limiting their usability to mature capital and constraining price reliability. Platforms that successfully combine strong retail channels with clear regulatory frameworks are most likely to raise the liquidity benchmark required for prediction markets to function as enduring financial instruments. In the medium term, the boundaries between prediction markets and traditional derivatives will continue to blur. Simple binary contracts are already functioning as forward-like hedges in specific scenarios, while leveraged prediction markets, credit tiering, and option-like structures are expanding the capital efficiency of event trading. While these innovations may accelerate market adoption (especially among institutional participants and high-net-worth individuals), they also introduce common risks from derivatives markets into the space. Artificial intelligence promises to further accelerate this evolution. As the number of markets continues to grow and become more granular, human attention is becoming a limiting factor. AI agents can continuously price micro-markets and scan for mispricing opportunities across platforms. Even in low-liquidity markets, low-cost and efficient pricing can be achieved through agents. Opinion markets, decision markets, and influence markets may further expand the functional boundaries of prediction markets. These models collectively drive prediction markets to shift from predicting "whether an event will happen" to pricing "what an event means." This shift will expand their applicability in portfolio construction and decision-making. Looking ahead, the most likely outcome will be a modular prediction market ecosystem. Regulated exchanges, on-chain protocols, wrappers, terminals, bots, and AI-driven interfaces will all contribute to the field's maturation, each optimized for different users and risk appetites. Prediction markets will gradually converge, ultimately establishing their enduring role as the financial infrastructure for trading uncertainty itself.

data.title: Circle CEO Predicts AI Agents Will Rely on Stablecoins for Fast and Global Payments
data.description:
Imagine a global marketplace that never sleeps, where the primary customers are not humans, but billions of autonomous software programs.

At the World Economic Forum in Davos, Circle CEO Jeremy Allaire painted a picture of a world where AI agents handle everything from booking a holiday to managing corporate supply chains.

To keep this machine-driven economy moving, Allaire argues that the world needs a financial layer that is as fast and programmable as the code it serves.

A stablecoin offering in Davos - great session on what’s happening with stablecoins and the development of the internet financial system. pic.twitter.com/JFkYKIVqY8

— Jeremy Allaire - jda.eth / jdallaire.sol (@jerallaire) January 22, 2026

Will AI Agents Become The New Power Users Of Stablecoins

The concept of "agentic commerce" suggests that within three to five years, billions of AI entities will be transacting continuously.

These agents require a system that operates without the friction of human intervention or the delays of legacy banking.

Allaire stated,

“There is no other alternative, in my view, other than stablecoins to do that right now.”

He noted that traditional credit cards and bank rails simply cannot handle the micro-scale and high frequency of machine-to-machine payments.

The future will be paid for in Stablecoins.

Today, Circle CEO Jeremy Allaire confirmed stablecoins are the only system that can support billions of AI agents transacting at scale.

In a landscape where autonomous agents are performing millions of micro-transactions per second,… pic.twitter.com/zcK9ZcPycL

— Stablecoins.eth (@Stablecoins_eth) January 22, 2026

This sentiment is echoed by other industry heavyweights.

Changpeng Zhao, co-founder of Binance, believes blockchain is the most natural interface for AI activity.

He dismissed the idea that an autonomous agent would use a traditional credit card to settle a restaurant bill or purchase services, predicting instead that crypto assets will become the default currency for AI-driven commerce.

CZ: "THE NATIVE CURRENCY FOR AI AGENTS IS GOING TO BE CRYPTO." pic.twitter.com/2nYriD1beO

— The Wolf Of All Streets (@scottmelker) January 22, 2026

Even Galaxy Digital’s Michael Novogratz recently noted that AI agents are on track to become the largest users of stablecoins in the near future.

How Tech Giants Are Racing To Build The Machine Economy

The shift toward automated finance is already moving from theory to technical development.

Coinbase has introduced a protocol known as x402, which repurposes old internet status codes to allow AI to pay for things using stablecoins.

This system uses Ethereum-based authorizations so that software can settle bills without needing a human to click "approve" for every small transaction.

Earlier this year we announced x402.

x402 enables AI agents to pay for content and services as well as execute transactions using stablecoins.

In the last 30 days we’ve seen over $200M in annualized transactions across both humans and AI agents. 📈 pic.twitter.com/5y6SSQViKs

— Coinbase Developer Platform🛡️ (@CoinbaseDev) December 18, 2025

Google is also positioning itself as a central player in this new landscape.

The search giant recently revealed its Universal Commerce Protocol and Agent Payment Protocol 2.

By using Google Pay to handle US dollar transactions, Google aims to ensure that when a user’s AI agent goes out to buy a subscription or service, there is a seamless bridge between the agent’s request and the financial settlement.

Is The Banking System Safe From Stablecoin Growth

A major point of debate in Washington and Davos is whether interest-bearing stablecoins will pull too much liquidity out of traditional banks.

Allaire was quick to dismiss these worries, calling the fear of bank runs caused by stablecoins “totally absurd.”

He argued that while reward systems help with customer loyalty, they aren't powerful enough to disrupt national monetary policy.

“STABLECOIN FEARS ARE TOTALLY ABSURD” 🚨

Jeremy Allaire shuts down the narrative that #stablecoin rewards will drain bank deposits and destabilize credit markets.

According to Allaire, the argument is “totally absurd.”
Stablecoins don’t weaken the system — they modernize money… pic.twitter.com/olWlwVqOfq

— COACHTY (@TheRealTRTalks) January 22, 2026

To support his point, Allaire pointed to the $7.7 trillion currently held in US money market funds.

Despite these funds growing by $868 billion over the past year, the banking system has not collapsed.

He observed that the very nature of lending is changing, with more financing moving toward private lending and capital markets, which now drive a significant portion of US economic growth.

Can Digital Money Solve The Friction Of Global Trade

As the economy moves toward this automated future, Circle is doubling down on infrastructure.

The company is currently working on Arc, a blockchain specifically designed to handle the financial demands of agentic compute.

Introducing Arc, the home for stablecoin finance.@Arc is an open Layer-1 blockchain purpose-built to drive the next chapter of financial innovation powered by stablecoins.

Designed to provide an enterprise-grade foundation for payments, FX, and capital markets, Arc delivers… pic.twitter.com/Z8FHUls1xY

— Circle (@circle) August 12, 2025

For Allaire, the rise of AI is the ultimate catalyst for the mass adoption of digital assets.

He maintains that while banks have historically been the gatekeepers of money, the speed of AI requires a move toward internet-native, programmable dollars that can move across borders instantly.

data.title: After five years of fruitless efforts, has Neynar's acquisition of Farcaster SocialFi failed?
data.description:
Deng Tong, Jinse Finance

On January 21, 2026, Neynar announced the acquisition of the decentralized social protocol Farcaster, with the founding team shifting towards wallet applications. Farcaster founder Dan Romero stated that the protocol contract, code repository, official application, and AI token issuance platform Clanker will be transferred to Neynar in the coming weeks for subsequent operation and maintenance.

Why is Farcaster transforming? Why did it choose Neynar for takeover? Is the SocialFi track really dead?

I. The Transformation Beginning Last Year: Coming for Tools, Staying for the Network

Farcaster had already given the reasons for its planned transformation last December: the social track was not ideal, and wallets were the key.

On December 8, 2025, Farcaster co-founder Dan Romero announced that the platform would undergo a major strategic adjustment, abandoning the path of finding product-market fit with a "social-first" approach over the past four years, and shifting to a growth model centered on wallets.

The team stated that the rapidly growing in-app wallet functionality is currently the module closest to product-market fit, and they will adopt a "first use the tool (wallet), then build the network (protocol)" approach to drive user conversion on the wallet side and turn them into active protocol users. Romero emphasized that the Farcaster protocol remains open and decentralized, but the official application will focus on expanding its capabilities in the cross-domain of wallet and social networking. Furthermore, Farcaster has previously acquired the token issuance platform Clanker to strengthen its related product portfolio. The full text of the post is as follows: Farcaster Status Quo, December 2025 (This week I will be writing a series of podcasts on this topic.) Varun and I have been working on Farcaster for over five years. Our goal has always been to build a scalable decentralized social network protocol with 1 billion users daily.
