This is a challenge, and while we have made some progress, we still have a long way to go to reach our goal. From the outset, we firmly believed in two crucial things: full decentralization and product-driven protocol development. This led us to make pragmatic, rather than dogmatic, decisions regarding the underlying protocol and to iterate extensively on our application. We never aimed to please the ultimate proponents of decentralization. For the first 4.5 years, we pursued a social-first strategy. We released a sufficiently decentralized, usable version of the protocol, allowing multiple independent teams to build and integrate on top of it without permission. However, despite numerous attempts (and some brief growth spurts), we consistently failed to find a sustainable growth mechanism similar to Twitter's social network—meaning the product and market were not yet fully aligned. Earlier this year, we launched a wallet feature within the application. The wallet has grown rapidly, and we believe this is the closest we've come to product-market alignment in five years. But how does a wallet help a social network grow? First, every user who registers, deposits, and starts using the wallet also joins the protocol. The more useful the wallet, the more users will use the protocol. Second, since a social network already exists, it's easier to build social features for the wallet that leverage the protocol. Adding a wallet to a social network is far easier than adding a social network to a wallet. Finally, we believe the best way to increase the number of protocol users is with a "come for the tool, stay for the network" strategy. (The wallet is the tool, the protocol is the network.) Does this mean the protocol has now become a casino? Not at all. The protocol is a collection of various features, such as following, interaction, identity, and wallet. As a developer, you can choose the most useful features according to your needs. The system is open. Our app focuses more on the integration of wallet and social features, but other clients, such as Uno, Recaster, Cura, Base, Zapper, and Firefly, take different approaches. As a user, you can control your experience by choosing a client. If you still want to use the Farcaster app, you can customize your experience using the following feed and keyword blocking features (e.g., try blocking "$"). Following this, in response to some user questions, Farcaster reiterated the reasons for the transformation: If you missed the previous message, let me reiterate: We are focused on building a great wallet to attract more users. Every new or retained user is a new user of our protocol. We tried a social-first strategy for four and a half years, but the results were not ideal. Wallet users have been growing, so we decided to double down on this direction. This means that new features and product positioning will be centered around the wallet. If you don't like this direction: 1. Use other clients; there are many options on the market. 2. Develop your own client. 3. Consider using other social networks.

II. Why Neynar? How will personnel be adjusted?

1. Why Neynar?

First, because Neynar is the software platform for the Farcaster ecosystem. Most Farcaster products run on Neynar infrastructure.

Neynar's long-standing positioning within the Farcaster ecosystem has been very clear: it doesn't focus on a strong consumer-facing narrative, but rather provides developers with underlying capabilities such as Farcaster API, indexing, identity resolution, data synchronization, anti-spam and growth tools. Farcaster is the protocol layer, while Neynar is the infrastructure layer that makes everything "run and scale."

"A True Account of the Collapse of Decentralized Social Networking: From High Hopes to a Disappointing Exit"In fact, most projects in the SocialFi sector suffer from the following problems:First, decentralized social projects often rely on the spontaneous migration of developers and users, but ordinary users are already accustomed to the Web2 centralized social model, and very few can truly migrate and become loyal users. Perhaps young people who are just starting to use social platforms are the user group that should be the focus of exploration.Second, compared with the frequent viral topics of centralized social platforms, Web3 decentralized social platforms attract more technical users than ordinary users. Once the content output is insufficient and not eye-catching enough, it is difficult to retain users. Finally, most decentralized social projects initially attracted users through airdrops and points systems. Once the incentive mechanism becomes unsustainable, the user surge driven by speculation inevitably fades. No matter how much hype and promotion these projects generate initially, they ultimately struggle to solve the problem of user churn. Conclusion In summary, judging from the current state of SocialFi projects, the decentralized social sector has indeed experienced a downturn. However, this may only be a temporary setback. If a truly practical application can be developed that provides users with genuine content value and combines earning and transfer functions, it might ignite a genuine decentralized social boom in the Web3 space. This is precisely the core logic behind Farcaster's shift to the wallet sector—leveraging high-frequency wallet tools to drive user growth, and then using user retention to support the social protocol ecosystem. Decentralized social is not a false proposition, but merely focusing on "social priority" and using Web2 thinking to envision the future of Web3 projects may be the crux of the problem.

data.title: Predicting the future: Ten key signs of change in the crypto world in 2026.
data.description:
2026 is gradually becoming one of the most pivotal years in the history of the cryptocurrency market. After years of innovation, regulatory changes, and shifting investor sentiment, the digital asset ecosystem is entering what many analysts call a completely new cycle. Unlike past cycles driven almost entirely by price speculation, the next phase appears to be rooted in deeper structural developments: Institutional adoption; Regulatory clarity; Real-world blockchain integration; Mature financial infrastructure. Below are the top ten cryptocurrency predictions for 2026, revealing where the market might be heading. This article discusses why this cycle might be different from anything you've seen before. Bitcoin may break the old cycle and reach a new all-time high. Experts and research reports suggest that Bitcoin could surpass its previous all-time high set in 2025, reaching new valuation levels in 2026. While predictions vary widely, some analysts believe that structural demand, including institutional interest, upcoming ETF inflows, and scarcity, could drive Bitcoin to break past peaks and reach new highs. This prospect contrasts with the view that Bitcoin strictly adheres to the traditional four-year halving cycle. Instead, the current narrative focuses more on fundamental demand drivers that could underpin long-term growth beyond simple cyclicality. The potential for renewed price strength is rooted in macroeconomic factors and the growing participation of a diversified investor base.

Institutional investment is expanding and diversifying

Institutional adoption is a defining theme in the crypto space in 2026, with research showing that market interest is shifting from speculation to strategic allocation.

Professional investors, hedge funds, and traditional asset management firms are increasingly viewing digital assets as part of their diversified portfolios. Based on analysis of institutional allocation trends, many large investors now view crypto assets as a **continuous allocation** (typically 1-2% of a multi-asset portfolio), rather than a marginal asset class. This participation is expected to expand further, from Bitcoin to a basket of products and diversified crypto strategies balancing risk-reward characteristics. Institutional flows, ETFs, and regulated investment vehicles are helping to bridge the gap between traditional finance and the crypto market. Clearer regulation will drive wider adoption. One of the biggest catalysts for crypto growth in 2026 may be regulatory clarity. Global jurisdictions are working to establish frameworks for managing digital assets, taxation, and market infrastructure. In countries like India, the upcoming 2026 regulatory bill is expected to address crypto taxation and provide guidelines aimed at boosting investor confidence. A clearer legal framework reduces uncertainty and risk, making it easier for institutional capital and everyday investors to participate. As rules regarding trading, reporting, and stablecoin issuers become clearer, market legitimacy and accessibility are likely to improve, accelerating mainstream adoption. Stablecoins are evolving into everyday financial instruments. Cryptocurrencies pegged to fiat currencies—stablecoins—are poised for significant progress in 2026. The use cases for stablecoins extend far beyond trading and speculation; they are increasingly seen as practical tools for payments, settlements, and cross-border value transfers. The United States has further legalized these assets through stablecoin-oriented legislation such as the GENIUS Act, allowing regulated institutions to issue stablecoins backed by high-quality collateral. This evolution could make stablecoins an integral part of digital payment systems, connecting fiat currencies and blockchain environments. They are expected to see wider adoption in retail commerce, corporate settlements, and even savings mechanisms this year. Real-world assets are gaining attention. Real-world assets (RWAs) are projected to be one of the most transformative trends of 2026. RWAs convert tangible financial instruments, such as bonds, real estate, or stocks, into digital tokens on the blockchain, enabling fragmented ownership, instant settlement, and greater liquidity. Large financial institutions are exploring blockchain-based trading platforms to support tokenized securities, and regulatory initiatives in this direction are underway. RWA can unlock significant capital by making traditionally illiquid assets more accessible to a wider range of investors. This, in turn, could drive demand for blockchain infrastructure in financial markets and reshape how assets are traded globally. Ethereum is consolidating its core role not only as a smart contract platform but also as a central hub for DeFi, RWA, and institutional use cases. Increased on-chain activity, shrinking liquidity supply, and rising staking participation all suggest that Ethereum may perform strongly in the coming year.

Staking ETH

(Source: Beaconcha.in)

Many analysts believe that in 2026, these factors, coupled with Ethereum's extensive developer ecosystem, may support its deeper integration with traditional finance and institutional capital. Ethereum's growing use in decentralized lending, borrowing, and tokenized asset markets could make it one of the most widely used blockchains by retail and professional investors. Prediction Markets and New Interfaces Expand Liquidity New forms of decentralized interfaces, such as prediction market platforms, are expected to gain momentum in 2026. These platforms allow users to trade contracts based on real-world events and outcomes, creating new ways to capture market sentiment and facilitate liquidity beyond traditional spot and derivatives markets. Research suggests that changing tax considerations and market preferences may help prediction markets attract broader trading activity. As these markets mature, they have the potential to become a major component of the crypto infrastructure, linking price discovery mechanisms to real economic incentives and expanding the ways investors interact with digital assets.

（Source: Coinbase 2026 Market Outlook）

Crypto Markets Remain Volatile but More Selective

data.title: Bitcoin can't wait 20 years; the quantum threat is already looming.
data.description:
Author: Youssef El Maddarsi Source: cointelegraph Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Bitcoin's 20-year quantum security window has collapsed. 25% of the Bitcoin supply is stored in high-risk addresses and urgently needs to be migrated.

Some Bitcoin supporters believe that the network will not face a substantial quantum threat in the near future—they cite the post-quantum standard approved by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), claiming that Bitcoin can be upgraded before the advent of quantum computers capable of breaking cryptography. This confidence is based on a high-risk assumption: the quantum threat will only truly arrive when quantum computers can crack keys in real time. Adam Back once suggested that Bitcoin had at least 20 to 40 years to prepare, but in fact, the quantum threat already exists.

Bitcoin cannot rely on a long upgrade path of decades.

Some readers may strongly object, insisting that the timeline for quantum technology development remains highly uncertain, and there's no need to rush into action; excessive warnings could even trigger unnecessary panic. However, the facts do not warrant our complacency. IBM recently achieved a major breakthrough in practical quantum computing. Its next-generation chips and faster error correction technology are expected to enable the company to achieve quantum supremacy by 2026 and launch an early fault-tolerant quantum system by 2029. This quantum technology race is intensifying. At the 2025 Devconnect conference, Vitalik Buterin stated that quantum computers may break elliptic curve cryptography sooner than expected, possibly even before the 2028 US presidential election, and advocated that Ethereum should transition to quantum-resistant cryptography within a few years. This contrasts sharply with the "security narrative" promoted by some Bitcoin enthusiasts, indicating that even the founder of Ethereum believes the window of opportunity for quantum threats is far more pressing than people imagine. I. Quantum Risks Already Have Market Impact A recent Deloitte report shows that approximately 4 million Bitcoins (25% of the total available supply) are stored in addresses whose public keys are vulnerable to quantum attacks. Researchers have long warned that sufficiently advanced quantum computers can use the Scholl algorithm to deduce private keys from public keys, allowing attackers to instantly empty traditional wallets. This is not a problem unique to Bitcoin. Ethereum and most current blockchains rely on elliptic curve cryptography, a foundation that quantum technology will fundamentally disrupt. Vitalik Buterin has developed contingency plans for scenarios where quantum computers could crack Ethereum accounts.

Second, the argument of "upgrading later" is not valid in reality

The core of the view that "Bitcoin has decades to prepare for quantum threats" is the belief that NIST's post-quantum cryptography (PQC) standard can be directly adopted before a substantial attack occurs. However, upgrading Bitcoin is not a simple patch fix, but a fundamental reconstruction of the protocol signature mechanism. Researchers at the University of Kent pointed out that upgrading Bitcoin to a quantum-resistant cryptographic system could require up to 75 days of downtime; if the network needs to reduce its computing power during the migration to reduce the attack surface, the downtime could even exceed 300 days. For an asset class worth trillions of dollars, a long-term global downtime is far from an acceptable "timely fix" solution for the industry.

Even if Bitcoin can achieve a smooth migration at the technical level, political realities will pose another obstacle. Bitcoin's governance culture has always resisted change, as evidenced by the fact that even the relatively mild Taproot upgrade underwent several years of debate and coordination.

The high-risk operation of forcibly migrating the entire network to a completely new encryption foundation will inevitably trigger ideological conflicts, potential chain forks, and long-term uncertainty. The idea that such a radical reconstruction can be easily implemented decades later ignores the adversarial game that Bitcoin faces in simpler upgrades. Meanwhile, the development of quantum technology is far exceeding most people's expectations. The European Commission and member states recently released a coordinated roadmap to transition the EU's digital infrastructure to post-quantum encryption, addressing the threat posed by quantum computers to existing encryption systems. The plan sets a unified timetable: all member states must launch national post-quantum encryption strategies and initial migration work by 2026; critical infrastructure and other high-risk areas must adopt quantum-resistant encryption by 2030; and by 2035, the post-quantum encryption transition of all upgradable systems must be completed. III. The Market Impact of Delayed Transition Could Be Catastrophic For the encryption industry, the urgency of this threat is also reflected in the potential market shock caused by an improper transition. If attackers use quantum hardware to crack private keys from dormant Bitcoin wallets, they could suddenly transfer millions of long-dormant Bitcoins, dumping them on exchanges and causing a price crash. Similarly, if malicious quantum miners can continuously solve Bitcoin's proof-of-work problem, it will undermine the decentralized nature of mining, turning this global industry into an oligopoly dominated by quantum technology holders. These risks will completely reshape the market landscape before the so-called "20-40 year security window" arrives. Post-quantum cryptography is inevitable, but it must be deployed before attackers acquire the necessary hardware, not as a post-hoc remedy. The NIST standard provides a roadmap, but not absolute guarantees. The transition will be lengthy, controversial, and disruptive. Attempting to postpone it for decades could expose Bitcoin and the entire crypto ecosystem to the most severe security challenges of this century. For the past 15 years, the crypto industry has been defending decentralization, trustlessness, and user sovereignty. Now, quantum computing presents a new challenge: will the industry act proactively, or wait for a crisis to erupt before reacting passively? The cost of misjudging far outweighs the cost of prior preparation. Many might believe Bitcoin still has decades of buffer time, but evidence points to a completely different conclusion: the clock of the quantum threat is already ticking, and the market is quietly adjusting. The only question is whether the industry can act before time runs out.

data.title: CZ's full Davos interview: Bullish on tokenization, payments, and artificial intelligence.
data.description:
Compiled by: CoolFish, Source: @kricsp

an

Moderator: Over the past few days at the forum, I felt that almost two very different dialogues were taking place here. One was a discussion about geopolitics and macroeconomics, concerns about the fragmentation of the global trading system, debt, and the role of the US dollar. These dialogues were often very frustrating and worrying. The other focus was on discussions about artificial intelligence, innovation, and technology. These discussions about future possibilities were vibrant, and many exciting technological breakthroughs are taking place.

In today's seminar, we will take a bold step by merging these diverse discussions—focusing not only on how innovation occurs and what its future holds, but also on the regulatory and geopolitical context in which these innovations occur, and how these contexts influence financial innovation. We have four distinguished guests. First, we have Steven van Rijswijk, CEO of ING Group from the Netherlands. Next, Jayee Koffey, Chief Enabler and Global Affairs Officer of BNY Mellon. Then, Fred Hu, Founder, Chairman, and CEO of Primavera Capital from China. Finally, Changpeng Zhao, founder of Binance from the UAE—we usually call him CZ, so I don't have to struggle with that name anymore. CZ is now free to speak without any restrictions, and I expect he will deliver a particularly insightful presentation. The theme of this discussion is how technology is reshaping the global financial landscape. Those who follow this field should be aware of the numerous changes currently taking place. Payment methods and monetary systems are undergoing dramatic transformations, and the underlying infrastructure for financial transactions is rapidly iterating. These changes are overwhelming. To begin the discussion, I will ask each of you in turn, seated in order: What do you see as the most profound restructuring? What development direction excites you the most? This could be a specific market sector—private lending, investment, Bitcoin, stablecoins, digital currencies, etc.; it could be a technological breakthrough—Bitcoin ledger technology; or it could be an innovation at the infrastructure level. The discussion is completely open; please speak freely. Please discuss which exciting new developments you believe will have the most profound impact on the financial sector. CZ: Absolutely. I focus on a very narrow area within the financial markets, primarily cryptocurrencies, blockchain, Web3, whatever you call it. I'm convinced this technology is a game-changer. And I think we've proven it won't disappear over the past 15 or 16 years. Binance is one of the world's largest cryptocurrency exchanges. Fred Hu: It really is the largest. CZ: It's the largest to date, larger than the top five exchanges combined. But look at some numbers—Binance has 300 million users. It's probably bigger than any bank I know. Its trading volume not only surpasses the Shanghai Stock Exchange, but last year it even surpassed the New York Stock Exchange. But currently, the truly mature industry in the crypto space consists of only two major sectors: exchanges and stablecoins, both of which are massive business systems. I'm very excited about three other new areas. I think tokenization is a huge area—I'm discussing asset tokenization solutions with more than a dozen governments, which can first realize financial benefits and then drive the upgrading of industries such as mining and trading markets. In the payments field, we've tried but haven't truly conquered it—to be precise, cryptocurrency hasn't truly entered the payments field yet. We've tried, but nobody actually uses cryptocurrency for payments. However, we're now seeing traditional payment methods quietly merging with crypto technology: when consumers swipe their cards, cryptocurrency is deducted from their accounts, while merchants receive settlements in fiat currencies like USD or EUR. Once these bridges are built, the payments field will see a major breakthrough. The third area, as you've all mentioned, is artificial intelligence. The native currency for AI agents will be cryptocurrency. Cryptocurrency blockchain will become the most native technical interface for AI agents. Current AI is far from being a smart agent; it can't buy you tickets or pay for your meals—but when it does, all payments will be completed through cryptocurrency. Host: That's the best-case scenario, the exciting part. Now I want to shift the topic and talk about how, whenever we go through a period of innovation and experimentation, some attempts succeed while others fail. So I want to explore what might not work. Ten years from now, if we were sitting on this panel in Davos, what developments being discussed today might not even be mentioned anymore? They might be obsolete. I'll offer a few ideas to get the ball rolling. You're all very excited about AI. But there's also some research from MIT showing that while AI can quickly accomplish a lot of work, its output is rather mediocre. They call it "work slop." You can get about 80% accuracy, and if you think that's fine, then AI is great. But if you really want to pursue excellence and ensure absolute accuracy, AI might have limitations. There's also the experiment with Bitcoin. El Salvador is pushing Bitcoin very hard. This is a good approach—El Salvador relies heavily on remittances and doesn't have its own stable currency. Bitcoin was supposed to be the ideal choice for remittances, reducing transaction costs, but despite significant marketing and resource investment, actual adoption is near zero. These are just a few examples of potential limitations for your consideration. Now let's continue. In the same order. What are some areas that people are excited about today, or at least some are enthusiastic about—but you think won't even be discussed in ten years? CZ, which areas should we be wary of right now? Or which areas are not worth investing in? CZ: Absolutely. I think all three of my guests are very tactful and politically correct. I'll give a more direct answer, which might offend more people, including those in my industry. I do agree with Stephen's point that if you asked me this question 10 years ago, I might have said Bitcoin payments. But ten years later, we're still far from that goal. So I remain skeptical of the payments sector. We are working on it. The entire industry is investing in numerous payment projects. But any innovative field comes with a very high failure rate, and the few success stories will grow exponentially, right? I also agree with Stephen's view on the metaverse. Okay, we've seen NFTs go through a really hot phase, but now they're pretty much dead. I have a strong feeling that memes might follow a similar path. I might be wrong. Many people in the crypto world will hate me for saying this. But, you know, there's a very high risk in new areas where value is highly speculative. Building use cases for these areas is difficult. Some memes have indeed survived, like Dogecoin, which has been around for about 15 years. So projects with cultural value may endure. But I think most memes won't last. And then I'd like to add, although this might offend other industries, I think physical banks will significantly decrease over the next 10 years. CZ: Yes, it will decrease. The need for people to go to physical banks will decrease significantly. I think ING pioneered online banking 25 years ago. It's clear that replacing traditional industries takes a long time. But today we have cryptocurrency and blockchain technology. Electronic KYC and e-everything already meet the needs of financial services, and the necessity of physical banks is decreasing. I don't think banks will disappear. They play a vital role—in fact, multiple vital roles. But all industries, whether emerging or traditional, carry risks. We should regularly and prudently assess market dynamics. Fred Hu: But you've only told us what failed in the last 10 years. You haven't told us what will fail in 10 years. CZ: Basically, you're saying that memes seem high-risk. Real banks are high-risk. I could go on, but that would offend even more people. Guest: Yeah. Host: No, I think your last point is very important. I wanted you to talk about banks. If you involve macroeconomists in this discussion, they'll be very concerned about the risks banks face. While banks remain a key source of financing for investment and growth—especially for SMEs, particularly in Europe, and less so in other parts of the world—the role of banks may weaken as new financial models emerge, and funding channels may shift to other mechanisms. Therefore, I'd especially like to hear your further elaboration on the risks. Another major risk that macroeconomists and financial experts focus on is the risk of financial markets. Everything is much faster now. Last week I attended a seminar on artificial intelligence and the risks of algorithmic trading in financial markets. I believe the pace of events has indeed accelerated dramatically. For me, the Silicon Valley Bank incident served as a wake-up call long before the many innovations discussed. In the Silicon Valley Bank incident, we witnessed a bank collapse faster than ever before—even compared to the collapse of Washington Mutual during the peak of the 2008 global financial crisis. Those two banks experienced a run of about two to three weeks before finally failing, while Silicon Valley Bank lost 80% to 90% of its deposits in just one or two days. That two- to three-week run only resulted in a loss of about 10% to 15% of deposits. Silicon Valley Bank lost 80% to 90% of its deposits in just one or two days. The pace of events is far faster than ever before. More importantly, this crisis wasn't caused by emerging technologies like artificial intelligence or Bitcoin, but simply by a new trend arising from people chatting online. They don't all have to go to coffee shops to exchange news and rumors. And because of new technologies, people can withdraw money from their online accounts without even having to queue. So in a sense, that's outdated technology compared to what you're talking about. However, this has fundamentally changed the speed at which bank runs occur. When you consider the impact on the broader financial markets, you see much more momentum trading. When everyone is trading using the same algorithms, with AI doing the work so that humans don't even have time to press a button, this will trigger more severe losses, more dramatic volatility, and a whole host of risks. How worried should we be about this? Is there any way to address it?

CZ: I'd like to add a few points. I think it can be broken down into several separate points. I think the first point is that, all else being equal, faster and cheaper is always better. This in itself doesn't create more risk. Existing risks are simply made more prominent by the increased speed. But if banks implement a fractional reserve system, when funds are insufficient, people withdrawing funds faster will only accelerate the exposure of problems. But slowing down doesn't solve the fundamental problem; it only leads to more consumers being unable to withdraw money when they need it, getting stuck, so they're trapped. This doesn't solve anything. **Therefore, all else being equal, technologies that reduce costs and improve efficiency are always superior.** Regarding the doubts surrounding Silicon Valley Bank, we sense a completely different atmosphere of rumors within the crypto industry. The bank may or may not be facing difficulties. But our impression is that this bank is very friendly to cryptocurrencies. It may have been shut down in 2023 by "Operation Choke Point 2.0." Let me give another example of Binance: In December 2023, after the FTX crash, after the Luna and UST crashes, and actually after the Silicon Valley Bank incident, Binance's platform saw a single-day maximum withdrawal of $7 billion equivalent in assets. The system operated without problems. In that week, the withdrawal amounts were several hundred million, then a billion, then a billion, then 7 billion, then another billion, then another billion. During that week, a total of $14 billion in assets were withdrawn from the platform, and the system remained stable throughout. I don't know of any bank in the banking system that could withstand such a scale of withdrawals. The so-called "bank run" essentially stems from the design flaws of the fractional-reserve banking system. When a fractional-reserve system is adopted, liquidity problems arise. Ultimately, this is a system design problem, not an artificial intelligence problem. That's my point of view. Of course, artificial intelligence does have the potential risk of synchronized actions. But I think this is just the tip of the iceberg, and attributing all problems to this would be too simplistic. Host: Okay, so I have some different ideas. So the key lies in settlement, risk management, and regulation, including reserve requirements. Some work needs to be done internally by companies, but government regulation and national infrastructure construction are equally indispensable. We are very fortunate to have such a diverse panel of experts – each of you operates globally, with your companies rooted in different countries: the Netherlands, the US, China, and the UAE. Could you all discuss the importance of regulatory frameworks? I'll start with Fred. In particular, different countries have adopted different approaches to managing these risks. Fred, could you specifically discuss some of the different approaches taken by China and the US? How do government practices affect opportunities in these countries? CZ: I have a different perspective on this issue because we are in different industries. Personally, I think the regulation of the banking and securities industries is highly developed, mature, and similar in various countries. Of course, there are differences, which might be a simplistic interpretation for a novice. But cryptocurrency regulation is quite different – ​​current policies vary greatly from country to country. Frankly, Binance has about 22 or 23 licenses around the world, but most countries in the world today do not have a licensing system. We've also seen rapid progress in the US, but it's still in progress, right? Regarding market structure, the Genius Act was passed last year, only six or seven months ago. So it's an ongoing process. We've also seen many other countries, such as the UAE, which have implemented quite forward-looking regulatory policies, as well as Bahrain, Pakistan, and Kenya. We're happy to participate in the consultation process with them so they can at least engage in dialogue with industry participants. I served as a private advisor to some governments—although I'm neither a cryptocurrency expert nor a regulatory expert. But I was simply speaking from the perspective of market participants. Furthermore, there are some key differences in national policies during this process, particularly regarding capital controls. Many countries restrict capital outflows, with any excess constituting money laundering or substantial illegality (however defined). The US doesn't have this problem. It doesn't have such controls. Tax systems vary greatly from country to country, which directly impacts financial regulation—for example, should the tax be levied on unrealized gains or realized gains when the price of Bitcoin rises after purchase? Clearly, clearer and more unified rules would greatly improve the situation. However, I think a global regulatory body is difficult to achieve, although not entirely impossible at present. Different countries have different priorities, different agendas, and different considerations, so a unified global regulatory body would be quite difficult. We would love to see that, especially if that global regulatory body could develop a positive and relatively innovation-oriented regulatory framework. That would make the work of industry players much easier. But frankly, logically it should be—after all, cryptocurrencies are essentially the same across countries. We don't want to change with each country, so there should be a best-practice framework that we can implement. I've actually spent a lot of time trying to figure out what that is and how to work with different countries. Host: It's good to hear that, I agree. Now may not be the time to launch a new global international organization or a new global regulatory framework. That will be a tough battle. But that doesn't mean we shouldn't start thinking about what it would look like if the opportunity actually came, especially if we encountered some kind of crisis or major financial collapse. We might want to have some ideas and plans in place so that some solutions can be implemented when the time comes. CZ: A relatively easy thing to implement is a regulatory passporting system. Like once you obtain a license in one country, it can be recognized in other countries. This only requires some kind of agreement between the regulatory agencies of different countries. We have already seen some related discussions. I think this step is most likely to be implemented first. Creating new regulatory agencies or even global organizations like forums is both difficult to implement and time-consuming.

data.title: Amazon Set to Cut 14,000 Corporate Jobs Next Week as Part of 30,000-Role Streamlining Plan
data.description:
Amazon Prepares For Record Job Cuts As AI Reshapes The Workplace

Amazon is tightening its belt once again.

The e-commerce and cloud giant is reportedly readying a second wave of corporate layoffs, scheduled to begin as early as 27 January 2026.

This move is part of a massive internal restructuring aimed at transforming the company into a leaner, more agile operation that puts artificial intelligence at the heart of everything it does.

While the company manages a global workforce of 1.58 million people, these specific cuts are aimed squarely at the white-collar heart of the business.

Amazon $AMZN is reportedly planning to cut thousands more corporate jobs next week - Reuters pic.twitter.com/VwCwPtFv1e

— Evan (@StockMKTNewz) January 22, 2026

Why Is Amazon Trimming Its Corporate Sails?

The primary driver behind this shift is a dual focus on eliminating corporate bloat and leaning into automation.

CEO Andy Jassy has been vocal about the need to strip away the layers of management that accumulated during years of rapid expansion.

During a call with analysts, Jassy explained that the reduction was "not really financially driven and it’s not even really AI-driven."

Instead, he pointed to the company’s internal environment, stating, "it’s culture," and noting that when a company grows as quickly as Amazon has, "you end up with a lot more people than what you had before, and you end up with a lot more layers."

Which Departments Will Feel The Impact Of The New Cuts?

While the majority of Amazon’s staff work in the sprawling network of fulfilment centres and warehouses, this round of layoffs targets the offices.

Around 14,000 roles are expected to be cut next week, matching the scale of the reductions seen in October 2025.

Sources suggest the axe will fall on several high-profile divisions, including Amazon Web Services (AWS), the retail segment, Prime Video, and the human resources department, known internally as People Experience and Technology.

This initiative brings the total goal to roughly 30,000 corporate roles eliminated, representing about 10% of Amazon's office-based workforce.

Is Artificial Intelligence Taking Over Human Roles?

There is a slight tension in the company’s messaging regarding the role of technology.

In an internal letter regarding previous cuts, the company described this generation of AI as "the most transformative technology we’ve seen since the internet," claiming it allows for much faster innovation.

Jassy also remarked earlier in 2025 that he expected the corporate headcount to shrink over time due to "efficiencies gained from the use of AI."

By automating routine, repetitive tasks and using AI agents to write software code, Amazon is effectively redesigning job descriptions to require fewer people for the same output.

How Is The Company Supporting Departing Staff?

For those caught in the transition, Amazon has historically offered a window of stability.

Workers affected in the previous October round were kept on the payroll for 90 days, a period that is set to expire on 26 January 2026.

Amazon US announced on October 28, 2025, the layoff of about 14,000 corporate employees due to AI efficiency and over-hiring correction. Affected staff received early-morning text notifications and will get 90 days of pay and benefits.

This time allowed employees to either seek new roles within the company’s high-growth areas, such as AI development and cloud infrastructure, or find external employment.

While severance packages and transition assistance are generally provided, the specific details often vary depending on the region and the specific job function of the employee.

The Real Cost Of Corporate Efficiency

Coinlive views this restructuring as a defining moment for the modern tech landscape.

Amazon is moving beyond simple cost-cutting; it is actively auditioning for a future where human oversight is a premium rather than a standard.

By stripping away 10% of its corporate mind-share to solve a culture problem of bureaucracy, the company is betting that software can manage what managers used to.

This shift forces a difficult question upon the global market.

If a company can grow its bottom line while systematically shrinking its human footprint, does the traditional corporate career path even exist anymore?

Innovation is now being measured by how many people a company can afford to let go while remaining operational.
